questions.stem: For a $2 \times 2$ linear system $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = A\mathbf{x}$ with trace $\tau$ and determinant $\Delta$, the eigenvalues satisfy
questions.explanation: The characteristic equation is $\lambda^2 - \tau\lambda + \Delta = 0$, since for a $2\times 2$ matrix the trace equals $\lambda_1 + \lambda_2 = \tau$ and the determinant equals $\lambda_1 \lambda_2 = \Delta$. The quadratic formula gives $\lambda = \tfrac{1}{2}(\tau \pm \sqrt{\tau^2 - 4\Delta})$. The discriminant $\tau^2 - 4\Delta$ controls whether the eigenvalues are real or complex.
questions.source: in the style of Strogatz, Nonlinear Dynamics and Chaos

questions.stem: The fixed point of $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = A\mathbf{x}$ with $A = \begin{pmatrix} -2 & 0 \\ 0 & -3 \end{pmatrix}$ is a
questions.explanation: The matrix is diagonal, so the eigenvalues are $\lambda_1 = -2$ and $\lambda_2 = -3$. Both are real, distinct, and negative, so trajectories decay along two directions: this is a stable node. (Check: $\tau = -5 < 0$, $\Delta = 6 > 0$, $\tau^2 - 4\Delta = 25 - 24 = 1 > 0$, confirming a real stable node.)
questions.source: in the style of Strogatz, Nonlinear Dynamics and Chaos

questions.explanation: Diagonal matrix gives eigenvalues $\lambda_1 = 1$ and $\lambda_2 = 4$, both real and positive. Trajectories grow along both eigendirections, so the origin is an unstable node. (Check: $\tau = 5 > 0$, $\Delta = 4 > 0$, $\tau^2 - 4\Delta = 25 - 16 = 9 > 0$.)
questions.source: in the style of Strogatz, Nonlinear Dynamics and Chaos

questions.explanation: Here $\tau = 0$ and $\Delta = 0\cdot 0 - (1)(-1) = 1 > 0$. The eigenvalues are $\lambda = \pm i$, purely imaginary. Purely imaginary eigenvalues with zero real part give closed orbits, so the origin is a center. (This is the harmonic oscillator $\ddot{x} + x = 0$ in first order form.)
questions.source: in the style of Strogatz, Nonlinear Dynamics and Chaos

questions.explanation: Compute $\tau = 4$, $\Delta = (2)(2) - (1)(1) = 3$, discriminant $\tau^2 - 4\Delta = 16 - 12 = 4 > 0$. Eigenvalues $\lambda = \tfrac{1}{2}(4 \pm 2) = 3, 1$, both real and positive, so the origin is an unstable node.
questions.source: in the style of Strogatz, Nonlinear Dynamics and Chaos

questions.explanation: Here $\tau = -2 < 0$, $\Delta = (-1)(-1) - (-1)(1) = 1 + 1 = 2 > 0$, and $\tau^2 - 4\Delta = 4 - 8 = -4 < 0$. A negative discriminant means complex eigenvalues $\lambda = -1 \pm i$; the negative real part makes it a stable spiral (inward winding).
questions.source: in the style of Strogatz, Nonlinear Dynamics and Chaos

questions.explanation: Compute $\Delta = (1)(1) - (2)(2) = 1 - 4 = -3 < 0$. A negative determinant always gives a saddle, since the eigenvalues are real with opposite signs. Indeed $\tau = 2$, discriminant $= 4 + 12 = 16$, $\lambda = \tfrac{1}{2}(2 \pm 4) = 3, -1$: opposite signs confirm the saddle.
questions.source: in the style of Strogatz, Nonlinear Dynamics and Chaos

questions.explanation: Compute $\tau = 3 + (-1) = 2$, $\Delta = (3)(-1) - (-2)(4) = -3 + 8 = 5$, discriminant $\tau^2 - 4\Delta = 4 - 20 = -16 < 0$. Complex eigenvalues $\lambda = 1 \pm 2i$ with positive real part give an unstable spiral.
questions.source: in the style of Strogatz, Nonlinear Dynamics and Chaos

questions.explanation: Trace $\tau = -7$, determinant $\Delta = (-4)(-3) - (2)(1) = 12 - 2 = 10$. Characteristic equation $\lambda^2 + 7\lambda + 10 = 0$ factors as $(\lambda + 2)(\lambda + 5) = 0$, giving $\lambda = -2, -5$. Both negative, so the origin is a stable node.
questions.source: in the style of Strogatz, Nonlinear Dynamics and Chaos

questions.stem: A linear system has $\tau < 0$ and $\Delta > 0$ with $\tau^2 - 4\Delta < 0$. The origin is a
questions.explanation: A negative discriminant ($\tau^2 - 4\Delta < 0$) means complex conjugate eigenvalues, so the fixed point spirals. The real part is $\tau/2 < 0$, so the spiral decays inward: a stable spiral. Positive trace would give an unstable spiral instead.
questions.source: in the style of Strogatz, Nonlinear Dynamics and Chaos

questions.explanation: Eigenvalues are $\lambda_1 = 5$ and $\lambda_2 = -1$, real with opposite signs. Growth along one eigendirection and decay along the other is the signature of a saddle. (Check: $\Delta = (5)(-1) = -5 < 0$, which always means a saddle.)
questions.source: in the style of Strogatz, Nonlinear Dynamics and Chaos

questions.explanation: Compute $\tau = 8$, $\Delta = (4)(4) - (-3)(3) = 16 + 9 = 25$, discriminant $\tau^2 - 4\Delta = 64 - 100 = -36 < 0$. Complex eigenvalues $\lambda = 4 \pm 3i$ with positive real part give an unstable spiral.
questions.source: in the style of Strogatz, Nonlinear Dynamics and Chaos

questions.explanation: Trace $\tau = -4$, determinant $\Delta = (-2)(-2) - (1)(1) = 4 - 1 = 3$, discriminant $\tau^2 - 4\Delta = 16 - 12 = 4 > 0$. Eigenvalues $\lambda = \tfrac{1}{2}(-4 \pm 2) = -1, -3$, both real and negative, so the origin is a stable node.
questions.source: in the style of Strogatz, Nonlinear Dynamics and Chaos

questions.stem: On the trace determinant plane, a fixed point is a center when
questions.explanation: A center requires purely imaginary eigenvalues $\lambda = \pm i\omega$. This needs zero real part, $\tau = 0$, with $\Delta > 0$ so the eigenvalues are $\pm i\sqrt{\Delta}$ (nonzero and imaginary). The condition $\tau^2 - 4\Delta = 0$ is the parabola of degenerate nodes, and $\Delta < 0$ is the saddle region.
questions.source: in the style of Strogatz, Nonlinear Dynamics and Chaos

questions.explanation: This upper triangular matrix has a repeated eigenvalue $\lambda = -3$ (twice), and $\tau^2 - 4\Delta = 36 - 36 = 0$ sits exactly on the borderline parabola. Because the off diagonal entry is nonzero, there is only one independent eigenvector, so it is a degenerate (improper) node, stable since $\lambda < 0$. A star node would require two independent eigenvectors (a scalar multiple of the identity).
questions.source: in the style of Strogatz, Nonlinear Dynamics and Chaos

questions.explanation: This matrix is $-5$ times the identity, with a repeated eigenvalue $\lambda = -5$ and two independent eigenvectors (every vector is an eigenvector). All trajectories are straight lines into the origin, which is a stable star node (a proper node). Since both off diagonal entries vanish, it is a star rather than an improper node.
questions.source: in the style of Strogatz, Nonlinear Dynamics and Chaos

questions.stem: A saddle point in a $2 \times 2$ linear system is always
questions.explanation: A saddle has real eigenvalues of opposite sign ($\lambda_1 > 0 > \lambda_2$), so there is at least one growing direction (the unstable manifold). Any generic perturbation has a component along it, so trajectories leave the neighborhood: a saddle is always unstable, regardless of the magnitude of the stable eigenvalue.
questions.source: in the style of Strogatz, Nonlinear Dynamics and Chaos

questions.explanation: Trace $\tau = 4$, determinant $\Delta = (2)(2) - (-1)(1) = 4 + 1 = 5$, discriminant $\tau^2 - 4\Delta = 16 - 20 = -4 < 0$. Complex eigenvalues $\lambda = 2 \pm i$ with positive real part give an unstable spiral.
questions.source: in the style of Strogatz, Nonlinear Dynamics and Chaos

questions.explanation: The matrix is upper triangular, so the eigenvalues are the diagonal entries $\lambda_1 = 3$ and $\lambda_2 = -2$. They have opposite signs, so the origin is a saddle. (Check: $\Delta = (3)(-2) - (1)(0) = -6 < 0$, consistent with a saddle.)
questions.source: in the style of Strogatz, Nonlinear Dynamics and Chaos

questions.stem: If both eigenvalues of $A$ are real, distinct, and have the same sign, the origin is a
questions.explanation: Real distinct eigenvalues of the same sign give a node: trajectories approach (both negative, stable node) or leave (both positive, unstable node) tangent to the slow eigendirection. Opposite signs would give a saddle, complex eigenvalues a spiral, and purely imaginary ones a center.
questions.source: in the style of Strogatz, Nonlinear Dynamics and Chaos

questions.stem: Classify the origin of $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = A\mathbf{x}$ with $A = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 2 \end{pmatrix}$.
questions.solution: Because the matrix is upper triangular, the eigenvalues are the diagonal entries: $\lambda_1 = 1$ and $\lambda_2 =$ {{1}}. The determinant is $\Delta =$ {{2}} and the trace is $\tau = 3$. Since both eigenvalues are real, distinct, and positive, the origin is an unstable {{3}}.
questions.explanation: Upper triangular, so $\lambda = 1, 2$. $\Delta = (1)(2) - (1)(0) = 2$, $\tau = 3$, discriminant $9 - 8 = 1 > 0$. Two positive real eigenvalues give an unstable node.
questions.source: in the style of Strogatz, Nonlinear Dynamics and Chaos

questions.solution: The trace is $\tau =$ {{1}} and the determinant is $\Delta = (-1)(-1) - (-1)(1) =$ {{2}}. The discriminant $\tau^2 - 4\Delta = 4 - 8 = -4$ is negative, so the eigenvalues are complex. Because the real part of the eigenvalues is negative, the origin is a {{3}}.
questions.explanation: $\tau = -1 + (-1) = -2$. $\Delta = 1 - (-1) = 2$. Discriminant $4 - 8 = -4 < 0$ so eigenvalues $-1 \pm i$; negative real part means a stable spiral.
questions.source: in the style of Strogatz, Nonlinear Dynamics and Chaos

questions.stem: Find the eigenvalues and type for $A = \begin{pmatrix} -4 & 2 \\ 1 & -3 \end{pmatrix}$.
questions.solution: The trace is $\tau = -7$ and the determinant is $\Delta = 12 - 2 = 10$, so the characteristic equation is $\lambda^2 + 7\lambda + 10 = 0$. Factoring gives the smaller eigenvalue $\lambda_1 =$ {{1}} and the larger eigenvalue $\lambda_2 =$ {{2}}. Both are negative, so the origin is a stable {{3}}.
questions.explanation: $\lambda^2 + 7\lambda + 10 = (\lambda + 5)(\lambda + 2) = 0$, so $\lambda = -5$ and $\lambda = -2$. Real, distinct, both negative gives a stable node.
questions.source: in the style of Strogatz, Nonlinear Dynamics and Chaos

questions.solution: The determinant is $\Delta = (1)(1) - (2)(2) =$ {{1}}. Because the determinant is negative, the eigenvalues are real with opposite signs, so the origin must be a {{2}}. Solving $\lambda^2 - 2\lambda - 3 = 0$ gives the positive eigenvalue $\lambda =$ {{3}}.
questions.explanation: $\Delta = 1 - 4 = -3 < 0$, so it is a saddle. With $\tau = 2$: $\lambda^2 - 2\lambda - 3 = (\lambda - 3)(\lambda + 1) = 0$, so $\lambda = 3$ and $\lambda = -1$. The positive root is 3.
questions.source: in the style of Strogatz, Nonlinear Dynamics and Chaos

questions.stem: Use the trace determinant plane to read off the classification rules.
questions.solution: A fixed point is a saddle whenever the determinant $\Delta$ is {{1}}. When $\Delta > 0$, the sign of the trace $\tau$ decides stability: the fixed point is stable when $\tau$ is {{2}}. The boundary curve $\tau^2 - 4\Delta = 0$ separates nodes from {{3}}.
questions.explanation: Saddles occur for $\Delta < 0$ (negative). For $\Delta > 0$, stability follows the trace: $\tau < 0$ (negative) is stable. The parabola $\tau^2 = 4\Delta$ is the real/complex boundary, dividing nodes (above, real) from spirals (below, complex).
questions.source: in the style of Strogatz, Nonlinear Dynamics and Chaos

questions.solution: The repeated eigenvalue is $\lambda =$ {{1}}, and the discriminant $\tau^2 - 4\Delta = 36 - 36 =$ {{2}}, placing the system exactly on the borderline parabola. Because the off diagonal entry is nonzero there is only one independent eigenvector, so the origin is a {{3}} node rather than a star node, and it is {{4}} since the eigenvalue is negative.
questions.explanation: Triangular matrix gives a repeated eigenvalue $\lambda = -3$. $\tau = -6$, $\Delta = 9$, discriminant $36 - 36 = 0$ (on the parabola). One eigenvector only (nonzero off diagonal) means a degenerate/improper node, stable because $\lambda = -3 < 0$.
questions.source: in the style of Strogatz, Nonlinear Dynamics and Chaos
